disease.name: Toxic Megacolon
disease.note: A life-threatening complication characterized by total or segmental non-obstructive colonic dilation >6cm with systemic toxicity. Most commonly complicates ulcerative colitis (most frequent) and Crohn's colitis, but can also occur with C. difficile colitis (increasingly common in antibiotic-exposed patients), or other infectious colitis. In Kenya, amoebic colitis and TB colitis are important infectious causes to consider. This is a surgical emergency — delay in colectomy increases mortality. Surgery should not be delayed beyond 48-72h of failed medical therapy.
pathophysiologyDetail: Severe transmural inflammation in ulcerative colitis or Crohn's colitis triggers the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) that penetrate the smooth muscle layers of the colonic wall
Nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) is upregulated in the inflamed colonic smooth muscle, producing excessive nitric oxide which causes smooth muscle relaxation and loss of contractile tone
Destruction of the myenteric (Auerbach's) plexus neurons by the transmural inflammatory process disrupts coordinated colonic peristalsis, leading to functional obstruction and progressive dilation
Loss of the pacemaker function of the interstitial cells of Cajal in the colonic wall further impairs the generation of slow-wave electrical activity needed for coordinated contraction

Colonic dilation begins in the transverse colon (most dependent part in supine position) and spreads proximally and distally, with the cecum being the most vulnerable to perforation due to its largest diameter (Laplace's law: wall tension = pressure × radius)
As the colon dilates, intraluminal pressure increases further, compressing the microvasculature of the colonic wall and causing progressive mucosal ischemia and ulceration

Mucosal barrier breakdown allows bacterial translocation of luminal bacteria and endotoxins across the damaged colonic wall into the portal and systemic circulation, precipitating the systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)

Hypokalemia from diarrhea and poor intake exacerbates colonic atony by reducing the resting membrane potential of smooth muscle cells, making them less responsive to neural stimuli and more prone to dilation
Opioid and anticholinergic medications further suppress colonic motility by blocking acetylcholine-mediated smooth muscle contraction, precipitating or worsening megacolon in susceptible patients

C. difficile toxins A and B cause actin cytoskeleton disruption and epithelial cell death, leading to pseudomembrane formation and a severe inflammatory response that can progress to toxic megacolon, especially in hospitalized patients on antibiotics
In the Kenyan context, amoebic colitis (Entamoeba histolytica) can cause a particularly aggressive form of toxic megacolon with high mortality, often presenting with concomitant liver abscess

Free perforation of the dilated colon releases fecal material into the peritoneal cavity, causing generalized fecal peritonitis, septic shock, and multi-organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS) — mortality exceeds 40%

The vicious cycle of dilation → ischemia → bacterial translocation → SIRS → further smooth muscle dysfunction creates a rapidly progressive clinical deterioration that requires urgent surgical interruption
Colonic diameter >10 cm on abdominal X-ray is associated with imminent perforation risk — the colonic wall thins to <2 mm and cannot withstand further distension

landmarkTrials.trial: Oxford Criteria for Colectomy in Acute Severe Colitis
landmarkTrials.population: 136 patients with acute severe colitis (Truelove & Witts criteria)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Predictive model for need for colectomy based on day 3 response
landmarkTrials.outcome: Stool frequency >8/day on day 3 OR stool frequency 3-8/day AND CRP >45 mg/L predicted 85% colectomy rate. The 'Oxford criteria' remain the most widely used predictor.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established objective criteria for early surgical intervention in severe colitis. If Oxford criteria are met, urgent colectomy should not be delayed — day 3 is the decision point.

landmarkTrials.trial: Dutch Cohort Study on Timing of Colectomy
landmarkTrials.population: 107 patients with toxic megacolon across 8 Dutch hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early colectomy (<48h of failed medical therapy) vs delayed colectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Early colectomy: mortality 8%, postoperative complications 35%. Delayed colectomy: mortality 28%, postoperative complications 65% (p<0.01).
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the principle of early surgical intervention. Delaying colectomy beyond 48h of failed medical therapy doubles morbidity and triples mortality.

landmarkTrials.trial: C. difficile Megacolon — Surgical vs Medical Outcomes
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with severe C. difficile colitis requiring ICU admission
landmarkTrials.intervention: Early colectomy vs continued medical therapy for severe C. difficile colitis
landmarkTrials.outcome: Colectomy mortality: 35% vs medical therapy mortality: 55% (p=0.02). Colectomy survival benefit most pronounced in patients with lactate >5 mmol/L or WBC >50 × 10⁹/L.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated that colectomy provides survival benefit over medical therapy alone in severe C. difficile colitis with organ failure. Subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy is the procedure of choice.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line imaging in suspected toxic megacolon. Colonic diameter >6cm = megacolon. Loss of haustrations, thumbprinting, free air. Serial XRs every 12-24h monitor progression.
Best modality to assess wall thickness, edema, perforation, abscess, and alternative diagnoses. Essential when diagnosis uncertain or when perforation suspected.
CRP >45 mg/L on day 3 of admission predicts need for colectomy (Oxford criteria). Serial trend guides decision-making.
Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests tissue ischemia. >4 mmol/L is a marker of severe sepsis and predictor of mortality. Mandatory for risk stratification.
Mandatory in ALL patients with toxic megacolon. PCR is most sensitive. EIA for toxins A/B is faster but less sensitive. Empiric treatment while awaiting results if recent antibiotics.
Clinical scoring: >6 bloody stools/day + fever, tachycardia, anemia, or elevated inflammatory markers. 3-6 criteria = severe colitis at risk for megacolon.
Detects free intraperitoneal air (pneumoperitoneum) from colonic perforation. A negative study does NOT exclude perforation (supine film can miss small volumes).

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Recognize the Syndrome — Abdominal distension + systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension) in a patient with IBD or recent antibiotic exposure. This is a surgical emergency — time is colon.

Step 2: Confirm the Diagnosis — Supine abdominal X-ray: colonic diameter >6 cm (measured at the widest point, usually transverse colon). Loss of haustrations (lead pipe colon). Thumbprinting indicates mucosal edema.
Step 3: Assess Severity — Calculate Truelove & Witts criteria. Assess for danger signs: perforation (free air, peritonitis), septic shock, organ failure, lactate >4 mmol/L, colonic diameter >10 cm.
Step 4: Identify the Cause — History: known IBD? Recent steroid withdrawal? Recent antibiotics? Recent colonoscopy? In Kenya, specifically ask about amoebic dysentery and HIV status.

Step 5: Immediate Investigations — Blood: CBC, CRP, lactate, electrolytes, creatinine, LFTs, blood cultures, ABG. Stool: C. difficile PCR, culture, ova/parasites (ameba). Imaging: CT abdomen with IV contrast (if stable) or serial abdominal X-rays.

Step 6: Initiate Medical Therapy — ICU admission. NBM, NG tube, rectal tube. IV fluids (crystalloids, 30 ml/kg for resuscitation). Correct hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. IV steroids (Hydrocortisone 100 mg QID) if IBD. IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam + Vancomycin if C. difficile suspected). STOP all anticholinergics, antidiarrheals, and opioids.

Step 7: Early Surgical Consultation — Inform the surgical team immediately upon diagnosis. The surgeon should be involved from the start — not called when medical therapy fails. Discuss timing of colectomy with the ICU team.

Step 8: Monitor Response — Reassess every 12 hours: clinical status, abdominal girth, vitals, urine output. Serial abdominal X-rays every 12-24 hours. Repeat lactate and CRP at 24 hours. Failure to improve in 24-48 hours = colectomy.

Step 9: Decision for Colectomy — Absolute indications: perforation, uncontrolled hemorrhage, septic shock despite resuscitation. Relative indications: no improvement in 24-48h, worsening dilation, colonic diameter >10 cm, rising lactate, organ failure. Oxford criteria on day 3.
Step 10: Emergency Colectomy — Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy. Save the rectum for future IPAA (J-pouch). Peritoneal lavage. Damage control laparotomy if unstable. ICU postoperatively.

managementPearls.pearl: Time is colon — the window for medical therapy is 24-48 hours maximum. If the patient does not show clear improvement (reduced abdominal girth, falling CRP, improving lactate) within 24 hours, proceed to colectomy. Waiting longer triples mortality.

Do NOT perform colonoscopy or barium enema in suspected toxic megacolon. The insufflation of air during colonoscopy can precipitate perforation. Flexible sigmoidoscopy without air insufflation is acceptable for diagnosis in selected cases, but CT is safer.

Hypokalemia is a critical and correctable contributor to megacolon. Maintain serum K+ >4.0 mmol/L with aggressive replacement. Each 0.5 mmol/L drop in K+ significantly impairs colonic smooth muscle contractility.
managementPearls.evidence: Gan et al., J Clin Gastroenterol 2010

managementPearls.pearl: In C. difficile-related toxic megacolon, oral Vancomycin (125-500 mg QDS via NG tube or per rectum as retention enema) is superior to IV Metronidazole alone. Combine with IV Metronidazole. If the colon is too dilated for oral therapy to reach the mucosa, colectomy is indicated.
managementPearls.evidence: IDSA Guidelines 2021; Surawicz et al., Am J Gastroenterol 2013

managementPearls.pearl: Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy is the standard operation — do NOT perform primary anastomosis (unacceptably high leak rate in the acute setting). The rectal stump should be managed as a Hartmann pouch (stapled and left in the pelvis, below the peritoneal reflection). Place a rectal tube if the stump is inflamed for passive drainage.

In the Kenyan context, always consider amoebic colitis as a cause of toxic megacolon. Patients present with bloody diarrhea, right upper quadrant pain (from associated liver abscess), and rapid deterioration. Stool microscopy for trophozoites and serology (amebic ELISA) should be part of the workup. Treat with Metronidazole 750 mg TDS + Diloxanide furoate.

Postoperative management after colectomy: continue steroids in the IBD patient (stress-dose hydrocortisone 50 mg IV QID) to prevent adrenal crisis. The ileostomy will have high output initially — replace losses with IV fluids and monitor electrolytes. Start TPN if the patient is malnourished and unable to tolerate enteral nutrition.

The decision to perform proctectomy at the time of colectomy for toxic megacolon is almost never indicated. Keeping the rectum allows for future restorative surgery (IPAA) and avoids the morbidity of an acute pelvic dissection (ureteric injury, presacral bleeding, impotence). The rectal stump can be managed medically with mesalamine enemas.
managementPearls.evidence: Fazio et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1995

operativeNuances.nuance: At laparotomy for toxic megacolon, the colon is paper-thin and at imminent risk of perforation. Handle the bowel with extreme care — use a 'no-touch' technique and consider decompressing the colon with a large-bore needle attached to suction before manipulating it. A perforation during mobilization dramatically increases sepsis risk.
operativeNuances.evidence: Goligher et al., Br J Surg 1970

operativeNuances.nuance: In damage-control situations (hemodynamic instability, acidosis, coagulopathy), perform a rapid total colectomy, leave the abdomen open with a negative pressure dressing (VAC/laparostomy), and plan a second-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours. This approach reduces mortality in the sickest patients.
operativeNuances.evidence: Waele et al., World J Surg 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: During laparoscopic colectomy for toxic megacolon (selected stable patients), use a hand-assisted technique through a Pfannenstiel incision. This provides tactile feedback for the fragile colon, allows rapid decompression, and facilitates extraction. Pure laparoscopic approach is challenging due to the dilated, friable colon occupying most of the abdominal cavity.
operativeNuances.evidence: Marcello et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2006

operativeNuances.nuance: The rectal stump should be divided at the sacral promontory (upper rectum) — leaving a longer stump (>15 cm) increases the risk of stump blowout from ongoing inflammation. Suture the staple line with 3-0 absorbable suture to reinforce it. Mark the stump with a long absorbable suture for easy identification at the second-stage procedure.
operativeNuances.evidence: Fazio et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1995

operativeNuances.nuance: The ileostomy should be created in the right lower quadrant through the rectus muscle. Bring out sufficient ileum (5-6 cm above skin level) to allow a spouted Brooke ileostomy. The mesentery is often thickened from steroid use — ensure the stoma is tension-free and well-vascularized.

prognosis.naturalHistory: Toxic megacolon has a high mortality (20-40%) without timely intervention. With appropriate management (early medical therapy + timely colectomy), mortality decreases to <10%. The natural history is one of rapid deterioration over hours to days — progression from systemic toxicity to septic shock and multi-organ failure is measured in hours, not days.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After total abdominal colectomy for toxic megacolon, the disease is cured in the large intestine. However, the rectal stump remains at risk of inflammation (proctitis) requiring medical management. In IBD patients, 20-30% will develop clinically significant proctitis in the retained rectum within 5 years.
prognosis.cancerRisk: The retained rectal stump in ulcerative colitis patients is at risk for dysplasia and adenocarcinoma (similar to UC colitis-associated cancer risk). Surveillance proctoscopy with biopsies every 1-2 years is recommended. After proctectomy (second-stage), the risk of pouch dysplasia is low but surveillance is still recommended.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: An emergency colectomy with ileostomy is a life-saving but life-altering operation. Quality of life is significantly impaired in the first 3-6 months after surgery. However, most patients adapt well to the ileostomy, and after completion proctectomy with IPAA (J-pouch), quality of life returns to near-normal levels. Emergency colectomy carries significant psychological trauma — psychological support is essential.
prognosis.mortality: Overall mortality for toxic megacolon: 5-10% in specialist centers with early surgical intervention. Mortality with perforation: 30-50%. Mortality without surgery: >50%. Independent predictors of mortality: age >70 years, septic shock at presentation, lactate >4 mmol/L, colonic perforation, and delay in colectomy beyond 48h of failed medical therapy.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: delayed surgery, colonic perforation, multi-organ failure, age >70, hypoalbuminemia <25 g/L, lactate >4 mmol/L, immunosuppression (steroids, biologics), and high ASA score. Protective factors: early surgical consultation, ICU care, timely colectomy (<48h of failed medical therapy), aggressive fluid and electrolyte resuscitation, and appropriate antibiotic coverage.

presentingComplaints: Massive abdominal distension that develops rapidly
Bloody diarrhea that may decrease or stop as the colon dilates (paradoxical improvement)
High fever with rigors and feeling of severe illness
Rapid heart rate and feeling faint or dizzy

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Any perforation or failure of medical therapy -> SEVERE - URGENT SURGERY; initial presentation without perforation -> STILL URGENT with close monitoring

history_questions.question: Do you have a known history of ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease?
Have you noticed your abdomen becoming swollen over the past hours to days?
Have you had bloody diarrhea? Has it decreased or stopped recently?
Do you have fever and chills? Have you taken your temperature?
Have you recently stopped taking your colitis medications (steroids, mesalamine)?
Have you taken antibiotics in the past few weeks?
Have you taken any antidiarrheal medications (Loperamide, codeine)?
Do you have severe abdominal pain — is it constant or cramping?
Have you had any recent changes in your medications for colitis?
Have you had a colonoscopy recently? A recent biopsy or polypectomy?
Do you feel dizzy, light-headed, or short of breath?
Have you been able to pass stool recently? Is it watery, bloody, or have you stopped passing stool?
What medications are you currently taking for your colitis?
Have you tried any traditional herbal remedies?
Do you have any other chronic illnesses like diabetes or HIV?
Have you ever been hospitalized for colitis before?
Do you smoke? Have you recently quit smoking (UC patients often flare after quitting)?
Do you have any joint pain, skin rashes, or eye inflammation (extra-intestinal IBD symptoms)?
Have you had any vomiting?
Are you passing less urine than usual?
Have you been in hospital recently or taken antibiotics recently?
Have you taken any traditional medicines or herbs for your abdominal symptoms?
Have you been diagnosed with HIV or any immune system problem?
Have you travelled recently or had contact with someone with diarrhea?

examination_findings.finding: Tympanic percussion note over the dilated colon
Absent or markedly decreased bowel sounds
Dehydration — dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor
Confusion or altered mental status from sepsis
Right lower quadrant tenderness or mass (if associated with Crohn's)
Tachypnea and hypoxia from abdominal distension splinting diaphragm
Peritoneal signs in the right upper quadrant if cecal perforation
Oliguria — urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hr despite fluid resuscitation
Altered mental status — confusion, agitation, or obtundation from sepsis
Hypoxia — SpO2 <90% on room air from diaphragmatic splinting or ARDS
Skin mottling or cold peripheries — signs of distributive shock

bedside_scores.name: Truelove & Witts Severity Index (UC)
bedside_scores.interpretation: 3-6 = severe colitis at risk of megacolon → urgent surgical consult. Any patient with 3+ criteria AND colonic dilation has toxic megacolon until proven otherwise.

riskFactors.note: Less common than UC but still a risk

subtypes.name: Toxic Megacolon in IBD
subtypes.management: Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy — save the rectum. IV steroids (Hydrocortisone 100mg QID). IV antibiotics. Fluid resuscitation. ICU care. Surgery if no improvement in 24-48h or if perforation.

subtypes.name: Infectious Toxic Megacolon
subtypes.management: IV Vancomycin (oral if possible) + Metronidazole for C. difficile. Urgent colectomy if medical therapy fails or perforation occurs. Avoid anti-peristaltic agents. IV fluids, electrolyte correction. ICU.

subtypes.name: Amebic Toxic Megacolon
subtypes.management: IV Metronidazole 750mg TDS + Diloxanide furoate. Urgent colectomy if perforation or failure of medical therapy. Higher mortality than IBD-related megacolon. In Kenya, consider this in patients with dysentery, poor sanitation exposure, or HIV.

bedside.name: Abdominal X-Ray supine + erect
bedside.indication: colonic diameter >6cm, thumbprinting, loss of haustrations

bedside.name: qSOFA score (respiratory rate, mental status, BP)

laboratory.name: WBC count with differential
laboratory.indication: leukocytosis or leukopenia — left shift suggests severity

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: tissue hypoperfusion marker — elevated suggests ischemia

laboratory.name: Stool C. difficile toxin (PCR or EIA)
laboratory.indication: mandatory — C. diff is a common cause

laboratory.name: Stool culture and Ova/Parasites
laboratory.indication: exclude infectious colitis including amoeba in Kenyan context

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: low albumin is a poor prognostic marker

laboratory.name: Liver function tests
laboratory.indication: assess for hepatic dysfunction from sepsis

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (serial every 12-24h)
imaging.indication: monitor colonic diameter, free air, thumbprinting

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast (water-soluble if perforation risk)
imaging.indication: assess colonic wall thickness, edema, perforation, abscess

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray (erect)
imaging.indication: rule out free air under diaphragm

interpretation_rules.colonic_diameter_threshold: A diameter >6cm on supine abdominal X-ray defines megacolon. >10cm carries imminent perforation risk. Serial films are essential — increase in diameter is a surgical indicator.
interpretation_rules.loss_of_haustrations: Loss of haustrations ('lead pipe colon') indicates severe full-thickness inflammation and is a poor prognostic sign.
interpretation_rules.medical_therapy_window: Medical therapy (IV steroids/antibiotics) should not exceed 24-48h without significant improvement. Worsening or no improvement = colectomy.
interpretation_rules.ct_signs_of_perforation: Free intra-abdominal air, extraluminal contrast, or mesenteric gas are absolute indications for emergency colectomy.
interpretation_rules.c_difficile_consideration: In any patient with recent antibiotic exposure, treat for C. difficile empirically while awaiting PCR results.

management.protocolReference: ASCRS Guidelines for Toxic Megacolon (2021), BSG/ECCO Guidelines
management.initial: ICU admission. NBM. Large-bore IV access. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (crystalloids). NG tube for decompression. Rectal tube for passive evacuation. Correct electrolyte abnormalities (especially K+ and Mg2+). STOP all anticholinergics, antidiarrheals, and opioids. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Carbapenem + Metronidazole). IV steroids (Hydrocortisone 100mg QID or Methylprednisolone 60mg/day) if IBD-related. Treat C. difficile if suspected (Vancomycin 125mg PO QDS + Metronidazole IV). Close monitoring: vitals, urine output, abdominal girth, serial abdominal X-rays every 12-24h. Early surgical consultation — do not wait for perforation.
management.definitive: Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy — the standard operation. Rectal stump left in situ (Hartmann pouch or mucous fistula). Proctectomy is NOT performed in the acute setting. 3 approaches: open (midline laparotomy), laparoscopic (if hemodynamically stable), or hand-assisted. Surgery indicated for: perforation, uncontrolled hemorrhage, worsening dilation despite 48h of medical therapy, or progressive organ failure.
management.postop: Continue ICU care. IV antibiotics for 5-7 days. VTE prophylaxis (LMWH). NG tube until bowel function returns. Ileostomy education and stoma care. Monitor for ongoing sepsis. Psychological support (colectomy with stoma in acute setting is psychologically traumatic). Plan for completion proctectomy and IPAA (restorative proctocolectomy) after 3-6 months if IBD.

pathways.criteria: colonic dilation >6cm, systemic toxicity, no peritonitis, hemodynamically stable
pathways.treatment: ICU admission. Medical therapy: IV steroids, IV antibiotics, NG tube, rectal tube, correct electrolytes. Close monitoring. If no improvement in 24-48h -> urgent colectomy.

pathways.criteria: pneumoperitoneum, generalized peritonitis, septic shock, organ failure
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy. Peritoneal lavage. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. ICU. Vasopressors. Damage control laparotomy with temporary closure if unstable.

operative_templates.procedure: Total Abdominal Colectomy with End Ileostomy
operative_templates.indication: Toxic megacolon — failed medical therapy, perforation, or worsening dilation
operative_templates.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Midline laparotomy, careful entry due to distended colon
Inspect for perforation — note location and degree of contamination
Mobilize the right colon — white line of Toldt
Mobilize the transverse colon — divide gastrocolic ligament
Mobilize the left colon and sigmoid — white line of Toldt
Identify and protect both ureters throughout dissection
Divide the ileum 5-10cm from the ileocecal valve
Mobilize the colon off the duodenum and pancreas (careful!)
Divide the colonic mesentery sequentially — ligate vessels (ileocolic, right colic, middle colic, left colic, sigmoid vessels)
Divide the rectosigmoid at the sacral promontory
Oversew or staple the rectal stump and leave in pelvis
Deliver the terminal ileum as an end ileostomy (right iliac fossa)
Copious peritoneal lavage with warm saline
Place pelvic drain(s) if contamination present
Close midline laparotomy with tension-reducing technique

dischargeCriteria: Rectal stump — no discharge or pain
Follow-up for future IPAA or completion proctectomy discussed

patient_education.explanation: Toxic megacolon is a life-threatening condition where the large bowel becomes severely inflamed, loses its muscle tone, and dilates (stretches) dangerously. If it perforates (bursts), it can be fatal. It usually happens in people with ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease, but can also be caused by severe infections. The treatment is emergency surgery to remove the colon (colectomy) and create an ileostomy (stoma). This is a lifesaving operation. In most cases, after recovery (3-6 months), a second operation can be done to reconnect the bowel (IPAA or J-pouch).
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you have: severe abdominal pain, increasing abdominal swelling, fever with chills, shortness of breath, ileostomy not functioning for >8 hours, dark or bloody stoma output, excessive stoma output (>2 litres per day) causing dizziness or weakness.
patient_education.activity: After surgery, start walking as soon as possible to prevent blood clots. Avoid heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks. If you have a stoma, you can resume most activities, including farming and boda-boda riding — but ask your stoma therapist about a support belt.
patient_education.diet: Start with clear fluids, then soft diet. Eat small frequent meals. Your ileostomy will require dietary adjustment: chew food well, avoid foods that can block the stoma (nuts, popcorn, maize seeds, raw vegetables, mushrooms, dried fruits). Drink 8-10 glasses of water daily. If output is high, drink oral rehydration solution (ORS).
patient_education.medication: Continue steroids in tapering doses as prescribed (do not stop suddenly — risk of adrenal crisis). You may need to continue IBD medications. You will need daily vitamins (B12, vitamin D, calcium). Stoma supplies will be provided. Do not take traditional herbal remedies without checking with your doctor.
patient_education.followUp: You will be seen by a stoma therapist. You need to learn stoma care: how to change the bag, clean the skin, and recognize complications. In 3-6 months, after you have recovered and your nutrition is good, we will discuss the next stages: completion proctectomy (removing the rectum) and IPAA (J-pouch) or remaining with a permanent ileostomy.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure stoma functioning. Wound healing assessment. Stoma care teaching completed. Rectal stump — assess for discharge. Nutritional plan (dietician). Psychological support. Family and caregiver education.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check. Stoma assessment. Review histology of colon. Discuss long-term plan (IPAA vs permanent ileostomy). Stoma therapist visit.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Ensure adequate stoma output (500-1500ml/day). Nutritional counseling. Discuss steroid taper if applicable. Coordinate with gastroenterology.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery assessment. Discuss timing of completion proctectomy (typically 3-6 months). Assess nutritional status and quality of life. Stoma revision if needed.
after_discharge.3_months: Plan for IPAA or completion proctectomy surgery. Preoperative optimization. Consider pre-operative MRI/defecography for pouch planning. Cessation of smoking.
after_discharge.6_months: Undergo IPAA (restorative proctocolectomy) or closure of rectal stump if no plans for pouch. Long-term follow-up for pouch surveillance.

followup.when_to_return: Emergency: severe abdominal pain, vomiting, high fever, ileostomy not functioning, heavy bleeding from stoma or rectum, dehydration with dizziness/weakness, wound separation or discharge.
